He worked with both Umm Kulthum and Abdel Halim Hafez. His compositions span nearly 70 years of Arabic music history.
legendary_artists.legacy: The modern Arabic music orchestra — with its combination of Arab and Western instruments — was largely Abdel Wahab's creation

legendary_artists.name: Fairouz (فيروز)
legendary_artists.note: Lebanese, not Egyptian — but Fairouz is so central to Arabic musical culture and has such an enormous Egyptian following that she cannot be omitted. Her collaboration with Egyptian composers (including Abdel Wahab) and her songs that became pan-Arab anthems make her part of the Egyptian musical consciousness.
legendary_artists.description: Fairouz — the voice of the Arab soul. Her recordings have been played every morning on Arab radio stations for 70 years. She is the most beloved singer in the Arab world alongside Umm Kulthum.

cinema.overview: Cairo is the Hollywood of the Arab world — Egypt's film industry is the oldest in Africa (the first Egyptian film was made in 1923), the largest in the Arab world, and one of the largest in the world by output. Egyptian films, in the Egyptian Arabic dialect, created a pan-Arab cinema culture that made Egyptian Arabic comprehensible from Morocco to Iraq.

golden_age.description: Egypt's cinematic golden age — when stars like Omar Sharif, Faten Hamama, Hind Rostom, and directors like Youssef Chahine created a canon of Arab cinema. This era produced romantic comedies, social realist dramas, musicals, and epics that remain beloved across the Arab world.
notable_figures: Omar Sharif (عمر الشريف — international star: Lawrence of Arabia, Doctor Zhivago)
Faten Hamama (فاتن حمامة — 'the First Lady of Arab Cinema', and Omar Sharif's first wife)
Youssef Chahine (يوسف شاهين — Egypt's greatest director, Cannes Palme d'Or winner)

cinema.cairo_studios: Cairo's Nasr City studios and the historic Studio Misr (founded 1935) produced the majority of Arab cinema for decades.
cinema.modern_cinema: Contemporary Egyptian cinema has produced internationally recognized directors (Mohamed Diab, Marwan Hamed) and the Netflix series 'Paranormal' (based on Ahmed Khaled Tawfik's beloved horror books) brought Egyptian storytelling to a global streaming audience.

television.description: Egyptian television has dominated Arab broadcasting since the inception of television in the Arab world. Nile TV and Egyptian state television broadcast across the region. Egyptian TV dramas (mosalsalat — مسلسلات) achieve enormous Arab-world viewership, particularly during Ramadan when networks compete with flagship productions.
television.ramadan_tv: Ramadan television season is the Arab world's most competitive and watched TV moment — major Egyptian productions premiere during Ramadan to massive audiences across the Arab world.
television.notable: Adel Imam (عادل إمام) — Egypt's most beloved comedic actor and cultural icon for over 50 years. His films and plays have defined Egyptian popular humor.

naguib_mahfouz.name: Naguib Mahfouz (نجيب محفوظ)
naguib_mahfouz.description: Egypt's greatest writer and the only Arab author to win the Nobel Prize in Literature (1988). His Cairo Trilogy — Palace Walk, Palace of Desire, Sugar Street — is the definitive literary portrait of 20th-century Egyptian life and the most important work of Arabic fiction ever written. Mahfouz was born, lived, and died in Cairo's old Islamic neighborhoods, which he transformed into universal literary landscapes. His assassination attempt in 1994 (stabbed in the neck by an Islamic extremist) left him physically impaired but did not end his writing.
notable_works: The Cairo Trilogy (Bayn al-Qasrayn, Qasr al-Shawq, al-Sukkariyya)

administrative_divisions.system: Egypt is divided into 27 governorates (محافظات — muhāfazāt), each headed by a governor (muhāfiz) appointed by the President of the Republic. Governorates are subdivided into districts (marakiz), which are further divided into city councils and village councils.

key_governorates.name: Cairo (القاهرة)
key_governorates.significance: Africa's largest city and the Arab world's cultural, political, and historical capital. Home to approximately 22 million people in the Greater Cairo metropolitan area (one of the largest urban agglomerations in Africa and the world). Cairo encompasses Islamic Cairo (the medieval Fatimid city — one of the world's greatest collections of Islamic architecture), Coptic Cairo (home to the Hanging Church, Ben Ezra Synagogue, and some of Egypt's oldest Christian sites), the pyramids of Giza (just across the Giza border), the Egyptian Museum (home to Tutankhamun's treasure), Khan el-Khalili bazaar, Al-Azhar University, and the modern business districts.
known_for: Egyptian Museum and Grand Egyptian Museum
Khan el-Khalili (medieval bazaar)
Al-Azhar Mosque and University
Islamic Cairo (City of the Dead, Sultan Hassan Mosque, Al-Muayyad Mosque)
Coptic Cairo (Hanging Church, Babylon Fortress)
Tahrir Square
Cairo Tower (Nile view)
Sayyida Zeinab and Hussein neighborhoods
Zamalek island (upscale residential/cultural)
Downtown Cairo (khedival-era European architecture)

key_governorates.name: Alexandria (الإسكندرية)
key_governorates.significance: Egypt's second-largest city and its Mediterranean soul — a cosmopolitan port city with a Mediterranean identity distinct from Cairo. Founded by Alexander the Great in 331 BCE, Alexandria was once the ancient world's greatest city — home to the Library of Alexandria, the Pharos lighthouse (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), Cleopatra's court, and the most diverse intellectual community of antiquity. The modern city retains its Mediterranean character — a long seaside corniche, café culture, European-influenced architecture, and a more relaxed atmosphere than Cairo.
known_for: Bibliotheca Alexandrina (modern Library of Alexandria — opened 2002)
Qaitbay Citadel (built on the site of the ancient Pharos lighthouse)
Alexandria National Museum
Montaza Palace and gardens
Stanley Bridge and corniche
Alexandrian seafood cuisine
Cecil Hotel (historic grand hotel)
Royal Jewelry Museum
Kom el-Shuqafa catacombs (largest Roman-era funerary complex in Egypt)

key_governorates.name: Luxor (الأقصر)
key_governorates.significance: The world's greatest open-air museum — the entire city of Luxor was the site of ancient Thebes, capital of Egypt's New Kingdom empire at its height (c. 1550–1069 BCE). The concentration of ancient monuments in Luxor is unparalleled anywhere on earth. The East Bank contains the living city along with the Karnak Temple Complex (the largest religious structure ever built — 30 years and 80 pharaohs added to it) and Luxor Temple. The West Bank — the 'Land of the Dead' — contains the Valley of the Kings (where Tutankhamun and Ramesses II are buried), the Valley of the Queens, the mortuary temples (Deir el-Bahari / Hatshepsut's Temple, the Ramesseum), and the Workers' Village of Deir el-Medina.
known_for: Karnak Temple Complex (largest religious structure ever built)
Valley of the Kings (Tutankhamun, Ramesses II, Ramesses III)
Luxor Temple
Hatshepsut's Mortuary Temple (Deir el-Bahari)
Valley of the Queens
Hot air ballooning over the West Bank at sunrise
Felucca sailing on the Nile

key_governorates.name: Aswan (أسوان)
key_governorates.significance: Egypt's southernmost major city and the gateway to Nubia — a beautiful, relaxed city on the Nile where the desert and the river meet in dramatic scenery. Aswan was ancient Egypt's frontier — 'Swenet' (the ancient name, meaning 'trade') was where Egyptian civilization met Nubia and sub-Saharan Africa. The Aswan High Dam (built 1960–1971, flooding the original Nubian homeland to create Lake Nasser) is one of the great engineering achievements and great tragedies of the 20th century simultaneously. The temples of Abu Simbel — saved from the rising waters by a UNESCO-coordinated relocation project — are 280km south.
known_for: Abu Simbel Temples of Ramesses II (280km south — world's most spectacular relocation project)
Philae Temple (Isis Temple — relocated to Agilkia Island)
Aswan High Dam and Lake Nasser
Nubian Village experiences
Unfinished Obelisk (ancient granite quarry showing pharaonic construction methods)
Elephantine Island (ancient frontier settlement)
Felucca sailing around Kitchener's Island botanical garden

key_governorates.name: South Sinai (جنوب سيناء)
key_governorates.significance: Egypt's adventure and diving capital — the Sinai Peninsula contains the Red Sea's world-class coral reef dive sites (Ras Mohammed, the Blue Hole at Dahab, the Thistlegorm wreck), the iconic beach resort of Sharm el-Sheikh, the pilgrimage site of Mount Sinai (where Moses received the Ten Commandments, according to the Bible and Quran), and the Monastery of Saint Catherine (the world's oldest operating Christian monastery).
known_for: Sharm el-Sheikh (premier Red Sea resort)
Dahab (backpacker beach town — world-class dive site: Blue Hole)
Ras Mohammed National Park (Red Sea's finest coral reefs)
Mount Sinai (Gebel Musa) — sunrise hiking pilgrimage
Monastery of Saint Catherine (world's oldest operating Christian monastery — UNESCO)
SS Thistlegorm wreck (one of the world's top wreck dives)

key_governorates.name: Red Sea (البحر الأحمر)
key_governorates.significance: Egypt's Red Sea Riviera — the stretching coastal resort strip centered on Hurghada (Egypt's largest resort city), El Gouna, Marsa Alam, and smaller beach towns. World-class coral reef diving and snorkeling, water sports, beach resorts, and dolphin and whale shark encounters.
known_for: Hurghada (Egypt's largest resort city)
El Gouna (upscale planned resort town)
Marsa Alam (quieter, world-class reef diving)
Giftun Island National Park
Coral reefs — among the world's most pristine

key_governorates.name: Matruh (مطروح)
key_governorates.significance: Home to Egypt's most beautiful Mediterranean beaches — with startlingly turquoise water. Also home to the Siwa Oasis (ancient oracle site, Berber Siwi-speaking community, vast date palm groves, natural hot springs, and Alexander the Great's consultation site). The World War II El-Alamein battlefield is here.
known_for: Siwa Oasis (Berber community, Oracle of Amun, Alexander the Great)
Marsa Matruh beaches (turquoise Mediterranean)
El-Alamein WWII battlefield and cemeteries
Cleopatra's Beach and Cleopatra's Bath (natural spring)

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: اللي يتجوز أمي أقوله يا عمي.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Whoever marries my mother, I'll call him uncle.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Adaptability and pragmatism — Egyptians are famed for their ability to make the best of any situation with wit and humor. Don't resist what cannot be changed.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: Patience is the key to relief (deliverance).
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Endurance through hardship leads to salvation. One of the most widely used proverbs in Egypt — a people who have endured extraordinary historical upheaval.
Cooperation and solidarity are necessary for any achievement. No person can accomplish great things alone.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: He who doesn't move around doesn't see.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Curiosity and travel broaden the mind. Those who stay in one place remain ignorant of the world's possibilities.
When choosing where to live, consider your neighbors before the physical house. Good community and relationships matter more than bricks and mortar.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: اللي اتعلم في الصغر زي النقش على الحجر.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: Illi itʿallim fi l-ṣuġur zayy il-naʾsh ʿala l-ḥagar.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: What is learned in youth is like an engraving on stone.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Early education and childhood experiences leave permanent marks. What we learn and experience young shapes us forever — particularly resonant in a country where children memorize the Quran.
What seems harmful may later prove beneficial. Setbacks contain hidden gifts. A philosophy of optimism in the face of adversity.
Love requires effort, sacrifice, and endurance of pain. Deep feeling comes with a price — but that price is worth paying. A romantic Egyptian wisdom.
Division weakens, unity strengthens — a universal truth with particular resonance in Egypt's complex multicultural, multi-religious society.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: A hint is sufficient for the noble/intelligent person.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: The intelligent person understands from a gesture or subtle indication — they do not need to be told explicitly. Subtlety and reading between the lines is valued wisdom.

proverbs_and_wisdom.proverb: إن غابت الشمس ما انسيناش الضوء.
proverbs_and_wisdom.transliteration: In ġābit il-shams ma nisinaash il-ḍōʾ.
proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: If the sun hides, we don't forget the light.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Memory preserves what is temporarily absent. Even in the darkest times, we remember and carry forward the light we once knew — hope survives through memory.

proverbs_and_wisdom.meaning: The hand of God is with the community.
proverbs_and_wisdom.lesson: Divine support accompanies collective action. Communal solidarity has divine blessing — one of the most powerful proverbs in Egyptian Islamic culture.

travel_and_places.note: Egypt is Africa's most visited tourist destination and one of the world's great travel experiences — combining 5,000 years of continuous civilization, some of the world's most spectacular ancient monuments, extraordinary Red Sea and Mediterranean diving, the Nile, vast desert landscapes, and the overwhelming vitality of Cairo.

must_visit.name: Pyramids of Giza and the Sphinx
must_visit.type: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Ancient Wonder
must_visit.description: The only surviving of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World — and the most recognizable monuments in human history. The Great Pyramid of Khufu (Cheops, c. 2560 BCE), the Pyramid of Khafre, and the Pyramid of Menkaure stand on the Giza Plateau at the desert's edge, visible from central Cairo. The Great Sphinx — 73 meters long, carved from a single limestone outcrop — gazes east toward the rising sun. Despite being seen in photographs millions of times, standing in front of the pyramids produces an overwhelming, reality-distorting experience — they are larger and more ancient than the mind can initially process.
must_do: Enter the Great Pyramid (Khufu's burial chamber via the Grand Gallery)
Visit the Great Sphinx at sunrise or sunset
Solar Boat Museum (ancient cedar wood boat buried beside the pyramid for Khufu's afterlife journey)
Grand Egyptian Museum (opened 2023 — the world's largest archaeological museum, housing Tutankhamun's complete treasure)
must_visit.insider_tip: Go at opening time (8 AM) to beat the crowds. The view from behind the three pyramids (looking back toward Cairo) is one of the most surreal urban-meets-ancient panoramas in the world — city skyscrapers on one horizon, millennia of desert on the other

must_visit.name: Luxor — The World's Greatest Open-Air Museum
must_visit.description: Luxor contains more ancient monuments than anywhere else on earth — the entire city was ancient Thebes, Egypt's New Kingdom capital. The Karnak Temple Complex alone is so vast it took 30 pharaohs and 2,000 years to build — its Hypostyle Hall (134 columns, each up to 23 meters high) is one of the most staggering architectural spaces in human experience. The Valley of the Kings — where 63 royal tombs have been discovered, including Tutankhamun's (KV62, the most intact tomb ever found) and Ramesses II's (KV7) — is an hour's drive across the West Bank. Hot air ballooning over the West Bank at sunrise, floating above temples and fields with the desert cliffs glowing pink, is one of the world's great dawn experiences.
highlights: Luxor Temple (stunning at night when illuminated)
Valley of the Kings (buy a ticket that includes 3 tombs; KV9, KV11, KV62 for Tutankhamun are highlights)
Hatshepsut's Temple (Deir el-Bahari — one of the most elegant buildings in the ancient world)
Valley of the Queens (Queen Nefertari's tomb — the most beautifully painted in Egypt)
Hot air balloon at sunrise over the West Bank
must_visit.insider_tip: Stay on the West Bank (in a guesthouse near the Valley of the Kings) rather than the East Bank — you can visit the tombs before the day-trippers arrive from Luxor city

must_visit.name: Cairo — Islamic, Coptic, and Modern
must_visit.description: Cairo is an overwhelming, magnificent, maddening, intoxicating city — one of the world's great urban experiences. It demands time and patience. Islamic Cairo (the medieval city) contains the greatest concentration of Fatimid and Mamluk Islamic architecture on earth: the Al-Azhar mosque and university, the Sultan Hassan Mosque (considered the most perfect Mamluk structure), the Khan el-Khalili bazaar (a labyrinth of medieval commerce), the City of the Dead (Al-Qarafa — a vast cemetery that hundreds of thousands of living Cairenes also call home), and the minarets that define Cairo's ancient skyline. Coptic Cairo — in the Babylon district — contains the Hanging Church (Al-Mu'allaqa), the Coptic Museum, the Ben Ezra Synagogue (where Moses was supposedly found in the rushes), and the ruins of the Roman Babylon fortress.
highlights: Grand Egyptian Museum (opened 2023 — Giza, worth a full day)
Al-Azhar Mosque and Mosque of Ibn Tulun
Sultan Hassan Mosque and Al-Rifa'i Mosque
Citadel of Saladin (Mohammed Ali Mosque — Ottoman alabaster masterpiece)
Egyptian Museum (Tahrir Square — until GEM replaces it)
must_visit.food_guide: Cairo street food is extraordinary — ta'meya sandwiches at Tahrir, hawawshi from a wood-fired bakery, kushari from a dedicated kushari shop (Hosni or Abou Tarek near downtown), and konafa from a specialist shop after dinner. The Cafe Riche and Fishawy's (Khan el-Khalili — 200-year-old café) are historic institutions.

must_visit.name: Abu Simbel
must_visit.type: UNESCO World Heritage Site / Ancient Monument
must_visit.description: The most spectacular temple in Egypt — and arguably in the world. Carved entirely out of a sandstone cliff by Ramesses II around 1264 BCE, the Great Temple features four colossal seated statues of Ramesses II (each 20 meters high) guarding the entrance. Inside, painted reliefs of extraordinary detail depict the Battle of Kadesh in vivid narrative. The inner sanctuary, 65 meters deep in the cliff, culminates in a chamber where four seated statues (Ramesses II, Ptah, Amun-Ra, and Ra-Horakhty) receive the sun's rays twice yearly — a feat of astronomical engineering. The Small Temple beside it was dedicated to Nefertari (Ramesses II's beloved queen) and the goddess Hathor. The entire complex was relocated in 1964–1968 by UNESCO in the largest archaeological rescue operation in history — raised 65 meters and moved 200 meters inland to save it from Lake Nasser's rising waters.
must_visit.how_to_visit: Most visitors fly from Aswan (35-minute flight) or take a very early morning road convoy (3.5 hours). Many visitors choose to stay overnight to witness both sunset and sunrise on the temples — the light at these times transforms the monuments.

must_visit.name: Siwa Oasis
must_visit.description: One of Egypt's most extraordinary and remote destinations — a large oasis depression near the Libyan border, isolated by hundreds of kilometers of desert on all sides. Siwa is home to the Siwi Berber-speaking community, vast date palm groves (over 300,000 palm trees), freshwater springs and salt lakes, and the ruins of the Oracle Temple of Amun where Alexander the Great came to be declared son of God (331 BCE). The ruined medieval mud-brick city of Shali rises dramatically above the modern oasis. The Cleopatra Spring (Ain Guba) and Fatnas Island are famous swimming spots. The landscape of sand dunes, palm groves, date orchards, and salt lakes glowing at sunset is one of North Africa's most beautiful.
must_visit.unique_feature: Siwa's Siwi-speaking Berber community maintains a culture distinct from mainstream Egypt. Their elaborate silver jewelry, traditional dress, and the ancient festival of Siyaha (when all Siwi men from outlying areas gather for three days of celebration) are extraordinary cultural experiences.
must_visit.insider_tip: Rent a bicycle or donkey cart to explore the oasis — motorized vehicles are restricted in many areas. The hot spring of Bir Wahed in the Great Sand Sea (accessible by 4WD) — a natural hot spring amid vast dunes — is one of Egypt's most magical experiences

must_visit.name: The Red Sea — Diving and Snorkeling
must_visit.description: Egypt's Red Sea coastline contains some of the world's finest coral reefs — with extraordinary biodiversity, warm waters (24–28°C year-round), good visibility (20–30+ meters), and a dramatic underwater topography of walls, wrecks, and reef gardens. The best dive sites: Ras Mohammed National Park (Sinai's southern tip — shark reef, yolanda reef), the Blue Hole at Dahab (one of the world's most famous dive sites — a 130-meter-deep vertical chimney, also one of the most dangerous), the SS Thistlegorm (World War II British cargo ship — one of the top wreck dives in the world), Elphinstone Reef (oceanic whitetip sharks), and the Brother Islands (hammerhead sharks, massive corals).
must_visit.for_non_divers: Snorkeling in the Red Sea is extraordinary even in shallow water — the coral gardens and fish diversity near the shore in Dahab, Marsa Alam, and some Hurghada areas are spectacular without diving equipment

must_visit.name: White Desert (Sahara el-Beida) and Black Desert
must_visit.description: One of the most otherworldly landscapes on earth — the White Desert (Farafra Oasis area) features surreal white chalk formations sculpted by wind erosion into mushroom shapes, animals, and abstract forms standing on golden sand. Camping in the White Desert under a sky undimmed by any light pollution — with chalk formations glowing in the moonlight — is one of Africa's most extraordinary nature experiences. The Black Desert nearby is covered in dark volcanic rock creating a completely contrasting landscape. Access is via Bahariya Oasis (4 hours from Cairo), with organized 4WD tours.

must_visit.name: Alexandria
must_visit.description: Egypt's second city and its Mediterranean heart — founded by Alexander the Great in 331 BCE, the city that was once the greatest in the ancient world. Modern Alexandria retains a Mediterranean character distinct from Cairo — its corniche stretches 20km along the sea, its architecture reflects Khedival-era European influence, its café culture is more relaxed, and its seafood is exceptional. The Bibliotheca Alexandrina (opened 2002) — a magnificent circular modern library built on the approximate site of the ancient Library — is one of Africa's most striking contemporary buildings. The Qaitbay Citadel, built in 1477 on the exact site and using stones from the ancient Pharos lighthouse, stands guard over the Eastern Harbor.
must_do: Qaitbay Citadel and Eastern Harbor sunset
Kom el-Shuqafa Catacombs (3-level Roman-era necropolis — most important Roman archaeological site in Egypt)
Seafood at the corniche (fish restaurants of Abu Qir bay)
El-Alamein Battlefield (90 minutes west — WWII Commonwealth war cemetery)
must_visit.insider_tip: Take the classic Alexandrian breakfast at a corniche café — ful medames, ta'meya, fresh bread, strong tea, and sea air

budget_tips: Egypt is excellent value for international visitors — meals at local restaurants cost $1–5, intercity train tickets are very affordable, and entry to most ancient sites costs far less than comparable attractions in Europe
The Egyptian pound has weakened significantly — making Egypt one of the most affordable travel destinations in the world for those holding hard currencies
Use air-conditioned intercity trains (Turbo-Train between Cairo and Alexandria; sleeper trains to Luxor and Aswan) — comfortable, inexpensive, and an experience in itself
Eat where the Egyptians eat — kushari joints, ful and ta'meya shops, and local restaurants serve better food at a fraction of tourist restaurant prices
Bargaining is expected in the Khan el-Khalili bazaar and markets — always counter-offer at 30–40% of the opening price and negotiate from there
The Cairo Metro is extremely cheap and efficient — the fastest way to travel across central Cairo and safe at all hours
Many of the most spectacular experiences in Egypt are free or low-cost: walking in Islamic Cairo, the Cairo corniche at night, Alexandria's corniche, and sunset at the Citadel
safety_tips: Egypt is generally safe for tourists — millions visit annually without incident. The main risks are petty harassment (touts, persistent salespeople), traffic, and occasional petty theft

The Sinai peninsula's northeastern areas (North Sinai — particularly near the Gaza border) have ongoing security concerns — check current travel advisories. South Sinai (Sharm, Dahab) and the Red Sea coast are safe and heavily visited
Egyptian traffic is intense — particularly in Cairo. Cross roads with local pedestrians, not independently. Do not assume that traffic will stop for pedestrians

Harassment of female tourists can occur, particularly in crowded areas — modest dress (covering shoulders and knees in Islamic Cairo and Upper Egypt) reduces but does not eliminate unwanted attention. Traveling with others is advisable in some areas

Food and water: drink bottled water only (tap water is treated but stomach bacteria adjustments can cause issues). Street food is generally safe if freshly prepared and hot — ful, ta'meya, hawawshi, and kushari from busy, high-turnover spots are fine

Sun and heat: Egypt's sun is intense year-round but extremely dangerous from May–September in Upper Egypt (Luxor, Aswan) where temperatures regularly exceed 45°C. Visit ancient sites early morning, rest midday, and visit again at sunset. Hydrate constantly

Ramadan considerations: during Ramadan, eating, drinking, and smoking in public during daylight hours is disrespectful and technically illegal for non-Muslims in some contexts — be sensitive to local custom

Photography: always ask permission before photographing people. Many Egyptians are happy to be photographed, others are not. Military installations, bridges, and government buildings should not be photographed
